rationale: The citing paper gives Iyer and Villas-Boas (2003) a specific role in its theoretical positioning, describing it as a bargaining model with a single manufacturer and single retailer focused on channel coordination. Dukes et al. then explicitly differentiate their contribution by moving to competing manufacturers and asymmetric competing retailers, i.e., a direct expansion of that bargaining setup. The citation also supports model-assumption discussion (asymmetric bargaining power), indicating functional methodological relevance beyond a passing literature list mention.
relation_description: The citing paper extends the bargaining-theory line represented by Iyer and Villas-Boas (2003) from a one-manufacturer one-retailer coordination setting to a richer competitive channel structure with retailer asymmetry. It uses the earlier work as a benchmark precedent for bargaining-based channel modeling while adding new implications for wholesale prices and profit distribution under retail cost asymmetry.
